text: The surgical team performs a right colectomy with complete mesocolic excision using a four-port system. The patient is positioned in reverse Trendelenburg with left side down. The procedure begins with the wide opening of the omental bursa through the gastrocolic ligament, revealing the Boucher area.

The surgical team performs a right colectomy with complete mesocolic excision using a four-port system. The patient is positioned in reverse Trendelenburg, tilted left side down. The procedure begins with opening the omental bursa through the gastrocolic ligament, revealing the Boucher area. The assistant manipulates the stomach to facilitate dissection of the peritoneal sheaths at the mesocolon root, preserving the gastroepiploic vessels. Dissection continues until the second part of the duodenum is exposed, identifying the head of the pancreas. The surgeon separates structures embryologically related to the stomach from those related to the colon, dissecting the plane between the mesocolon root and the middle colic and gastroepiploic vessels.

The assistant manipulates the stomach to facilitate the dissection of the peritoneal sheaths at the root of the mesocolon, preserving the gastroploid vessels. The dissection continues until the second part of the duodenum is exposed, revealing the head of the pancreas. The surgeon separates structures embryologically related to the stomach from those related to the colon, dissecting the plane between the root of the mesocolon and the middle colic and gastroploid vessels. Lymph node-bearing tissue at the root of the mesocolon is dissected, further exposing the head of the pancreas.

The surgeon continues the dissection of lymph node-bearing tissue at the root of the mesocolon, exposing the head of the pancreas. The superior layer of the transverse mesocolon is incised along the inferior pancreatic margin, revealing the right and left branches of the middle colic vein and the gastrocolic trunk. The superior mesenteric vein is identified, avoiding vascular and pancreatic injuries.

The surgeon incises the superior layer of the transverse mesocolon along the inferior margin of the pancreas, revealing the complex venous anatomy including the right and left branches of the middle colic vein and the gastrocolic trunk. The dissection follows the gastrocolic trunk to identify the superior mesenteric vein, avoiding vascular and pancreatic injuries. The procedure continues until the duodenal-jejunal junction is exposed, visualizing the third part of the duodenum while preserving the submesocolic pre-duodenal pancreatic fascia. The dissection reaches the plane between the Toad fascia and the Girota fascia beyond the external margin of the duodenal C-loop.

The surgeon exposes the duodenal-jejunal junction, revealing the third part of the duodenum while preserving the submesocolic pre-duodenal pancreatic fascia. A gauze is placed to protect the duodenal pancreatic complex. The patient is repositioned to a Trendelenburg position, tilted to the left, to begin the down-to-up phase. The ileocecal area is mobilized, developing an avascular plane between the right mesocolon and the vertebral peritoneal structure. The ileocolic artery and vein are clipped and divided, followed by dissection along the periaventricular plane of the anterolateral aspect of the superior mesenteric vein. The right branch of the middle colic vein is clipped and divided after removing lymph node-bearing tissue around the middle colic artery.

A gauze is placed to protect the duodenal pancreatic complex. The patient is positioned in a Trendelenburg position, tilted to the left side, to begin the down-to-up phase. The surgeon mobilizes the ileocecal area, developing an avascular plane between the right mesocolon and the vertoperitoneal structure. Lymph nodes bearing areolar tissue on the superior mesenteric vein are dissected, and the ileocolic artery and vein are clipped and divided. Dissection continues along the periaventricial plane of the anterolateral aspect of the superior mesenteric vein, reaching the root of the middle colic artery. Lymph node tissue around the artery is removed, and the right branch of the middle colic vein is clipped and divided.

The surgeon delivers the resected specimen through a mini laparotomy, extending the supraumbilical camera port site by five centimeters. An intracorporeal anastomosis is performed, followed by the routine closure of the mesenteric defect.

The surgical specimen is extracted through a mini laparotomy, extending the supraumbilical camera port site by five centimeters. An intracorporeal anastomosis is performed, and the mesenteric defect is routinely closed.
